What is the difference between a frame-off and frame-on restoration?

The restoration method significantly affects both the quality of the result and the vehicle's value. Understanding these terms is important for accurate extraction and valuation.

**Frame-Off Restoration:**
The body is completely separated from the frame (on body-on-frame vehicles) or the car is completely disassembled down to bare metal (on unibody vehicles). Every component is removed, inspected, repaired or replaced, and reassembled.

**Process:**
1. Complete disassembly — every bolt, wire, trim piece, glass panel removed
2. Body separated from frame
3. Frame media-blasted, inspected for rust/damage, repaired, primed, and painted
4. Body media-blasted, metal work performed, primed, and painted
5. All chrome re-plated or replaced
6. Engine, transmission rebuilt or replaced
7. All rubber seals, bushings, weatherstripping replaced
8. Wiring harness replaced or rebuilt
9. Interior re-trimmed (seats, carpet, headliner, door panels)
10. Complete reassembly with correct hardware, torque specs, and finishes

**Typical cost:** $40K-$200K+ depending on vehicle and quality level
**Time:** 12-36 months

**Value impact:** A documented frame-off restoration adds significant value — but ONLY if the quality matches. A poor-quality frame-off restoration can actually reduce value compared to an honest, unrestored car.

**Frame-On (Partial/Cosmetic) Restoration:**
The body remains on the frame. Work is done in-situ — painting over existing body, rebuilding the engine without removing it from the car, replacing interior components without full disassembly.

**Process:**
1. Partial disassembly (remove easily accessible components)
2. Body prep and paint (may be over existing body work)
3. Engine tune-up or partial rebuild (in-chassis)
4. Interior replacement (new seat covers, carpet, dash pad)
5. New chrome or polished existing
6. Mechanical refresh (brakes, suspension bushings, hoses)

**Typical cost:** $10K-$40K
**Time:** 3-12 months

**Value impact:** A clean frame-on restoration can produce a nice-looking car, but it doesn't address hidden issues (frame rust, body rot behind panels, wiring deterioration). It's appropriate for daily drivers but not for show cars.

**Concours vs. Driver-Quality:**

**Concours restoration:** Every component is correct for the car's build date. Date-coded parts, correct paint codes, proper finishes on hidden surfaces, correct assembly marks. Judged at events like Pebble Beach, NCRS, MCA, JCNA. Cost premium: 50-100% over driver-quality.

**Driver-quality restoration:** Looks good, runs well, but may not have 100% date-correct parts. May use modern components that improve reliability (electronic ignition, modern tires, upgraded cooling). Appropriate for cars that will be driven regularly.

**Extraction Cues:**
Listing language reveals the restoration type:
- 'Frame-off rotisserie restoration' — highest quality, frame-off with the body mounted on a rotisserie for complete access
- 'Frame-off restoration' — full disassembly and rebuild
- 'Body-off restoration' — same as frame-off, different terminology
- 'Ground-up restoration' — usually means frame-off or at least very thorough
- 'Nut-and-bolt restoration' — every fastener removed and replaced
- 'Cosmetic restoration' / 'freshened up' — likely frame-on, primarily paint and interior
- 'Mechanical restoration' — engine/drivetrain rebuilt, body may be untouched
- 'Older restoration' / 'restored in the 1990s' — may need re-restoration
- 'Show quality' / 'concours ready' — claims high quality (verify with photos)
- 'Driver quality' — honest assessment of a good but not perfect car
- 'Survivor' / 'unrestored' — never restored, original everything

**For Nuke's Valuation Model:**
Restoration type and quality should be captured as vehicle observations:
- Frame-off concours: highest valuation tier
- Frame-off driver quality: high tier
- Frame-on quality restoration: middle tier
- Unrestored survivor (good condition): potentially highest tier for certain cars
- Unrestored project: lowest tier
- Poorly restored: potentially lower than unrestored (cost to re-do the work)

The observation system should weight restoration documentation (receipts, progress photos, shop records) as high-trust data that significantly affects valuation confidence.

What is the Saab 900 Turbo and its emerging collector status?

The Saab 900 Turbo (1979-1993) is one of the most underappreciated collector cars on the market. A quirky, fiercely individual Swedish GT with turbo power, it represents everything that mass-market automotive homogenization has destroyed.

**Why Saab is Special:**
Saab (Svenska Aeroplan AB) was an aircraft manufacturer that started building cars in 1947. They brought aerospace thinking to automotive engineering: aerodynamic design, safety innovation, turbocharging technology, and a complete disregard for how other manufacturers did things. The ignition was between the seats (not on the steering column). The engine sat backwards (facing the firewall). The turbo was integrated into a front-wheel-drive layout when everyone else put turbos on rear-drive cars.

**The 900 Classic (1979-1993):**

**Variants:**
- **900 (base):** Naturally aspirated 2.0L inline-4. Modest power but Saab character. $5K-$12K.
- **900 Turbo:** 2.0L turbo, 145-175hp depending on year. The driver's Saab. $8K-$20K.
- **900 Turbo SPG (Special Performance Group) / Aero:** The performance 900 — 175-185hp, sport suspension, body kit, BBS wheels, leather Recaros. Only available in black, red, or white. The SPG is the collector 900. $15K-$35K.
- **900 Convertible:** Introduced 1986. The convertible is the highest-value 900 variant today. Turbo convertible: $12K-$30K. SPG convertible (if such a thing existed — SPG was coupe only): N/A.

**Why It is Emerging as a Collectible:**

1. **Saab is dead.** General Motors bought Saab in 2000, mismanaged it, and the brand died in 2012. No new Saabs will ever be built. This 'extinct brand' status creates a finality that drives collector interest — see also: DeLorean, Tucker, and other marques whose stories ended.

2. **Design icon.** The 900's 'hockey stick' C-pillar, wrap-around windshield, and aerodynamic profile are instantly recognizable. It's a design language that died with Saab.

3. **Turbo pioneer.** Saab was the first manufacturer to offer turbocharging across its entire lineup and one of the first to use turbocharging to improve fuel efficiency (not just power). The 900 Turbo's smooth, linear boost delivery was years ahead of the peaky turbo laggy cars from Porsche and BMW.

4. **Culture.** Saab owners are famously passionate and eccentric. Architects, professors, artists, and contrarians who specifically chose not to buy a BMW or Mercedes. This cultural identity adds to the car's mystique.

5. **Affordable.** Even the best 900 Turbo SPGs are under $35K. Compare to a BMW 2002tii ($35K-$65K) or Alfa Romeo Spider ($20K-$50K) — the Saab offers similar character at lower prices.

**Buying Considerations:**
- **Rust:** Saabs rust badly, especially in the wheel arches, floor pans, and around the battery tray. A rust-free Saab is worth 2-3x a rusty one.
- **Turbo system:** The Garrett T3 turbocharger is reliable and relatively inexpensive to rebuild ($500-$1,000). The APC (Automatic Performance Control) system — Saab's proprietary boost control — is unique and must be understood by the mechanic.
- **Direct ignition (DI):** The Saab DI cassette is a single unit containing all ignition coils. They fail with age. Replacement: $200-$400.
- **Parts availability:** Better than you'd expect. Saab's loyal owner community maintains extensive parts networks. eEuroparts.com, Skandix, and various Saab-specific suppliers.
- **Transmission:** The 900 uses the engine's oil supply for the manual transmission (the gearbox is integrated into the engine block). This means the transmission is lubricated by engine oil — unusual but it works. Gear whine is normal.

**For Nuke:** Saab 900s are uncommon but appear on BaT and Craigslist. Key extraction: differentiate 900 Classic (1979-1993) from 900 NG (New Generation, 1994-1998, built on a GM platform — less collectible). Identify Turbo vs NA, SPG/Aero designation, and body style (3-door hatchback, 4-door sedan, or convertible). The SPG should trigger premium valuation.

How does Nuke handle vehicle color classification and what challenges arise with non-standard colors?

Vehicle color classification in Nuke requires careful handling because the automotive industry uses thousands of proprietary color names that don't map neatly to standard categories. A 1969 Chevrolet Camaro in 'Hugger Orange' and a 2005 Lamborghini Gallardo in 'Arancio Borealis' are both orange cars, but their color names carry provenance and value implications that matter to collectors.

Nuke's color normalization pipeline works in three layers. The raw extracted color goes into the observation system exactly as found — 'Grigio Telesto Metallic' or 'Verde Artemis' — preserving the factory designation. A second pass maps this to a canonical color family (silver, green, etc.) for searchability. A third layer attempts to match against known OEM color code databases, which helps validate year/model accuracy since color codes are model-year specific.

The challenges are substantial. First, the same color can have different names across markets — BMW's 'Estorilblau' is 'Estoril Blue' in English markets but might appear as just 'blue' in a casual listing. Second, aftermarket repaints are common in the collector market, and a repainted car may be described by its current color, its original color, or both. Third, two-tone and tri-tone schemes (common on 1950s American cars and some Land Rovers) need special handling. Fourth, wrap colors are increasingly common and are temporary, so they shouldn't override the base paint record.

For auction data, color extraction is relatively reliable because auction houses typically list colors carefully. For marketplace scrapes, color data is far less consistent — a Facebook Marketplace listing might say 'blue' for what's actually a factory 'Sepang Blue Pearl Effect.' Nuke handles this by storing both the raw observation and the normalized form, letting downstream consumers choose their precision level. The observation confidence system also accounts for source reliability, so an auction house color designation gets higher weight than a casual marketplace listing.

What is the significance of production numbers in collector car valuation and how does Nuke track rarity?

Production numbers are one of the most important factors in collector car valuation because rarity creates scarcity, and scarcity drives demand among collectors. A 1967 Shelby GT500 with roughly 2,048 produced commands significantly more than a standard 1967 Mustang with over 470,000 built. But production numbers alone tell an incomplete story — survival rates, desirable option combinations, and market awareness all modulate the actual rarity effect.

Nuke tracks rarity through multiple data points in the vehicle record. The production_number field captures the total units manufactured for that specific year/model/variant when known. But the more useful metric is often the combination rarity — how many were built with a specific engine, transmission, and color combination. A 1970 Plymouth Hemi 'Cuda convertible is extraordinarily rare not just because few 'Cudas were made, but because the Hemi/convertible combination was ordered by very few buyers (only 14 in 1970).

The platform ingests production data from several sources: manufacturer records (when available), registry data from marque-specific clubs, and Marti Reports for Ford products. These get stored as observations with appropriate source attribution. When production data conflicts between sources (which happens frequently with pre-war and immediate post-war vehicles), the observation system preserves all claims with their sources rather than picking a winner.

Rarity interacts with desirability in non-linear ways. A car can be extremely rare but not valuable if nobody wants it — some low-production variants were unpopular for good reason (underpowered engines, undesirable colors, commercial fleet specifications). Conversely, some high-production cars achieve collector status through cultural significance (the VW Beetle, for instance). Nuke's valuation models account for this by weighting rarity alongside market activity signals — a rare car that rarely trades is harder to value than a rare car with active auction history.

For the extraction pipeline, production numbers are typically found in listing descriptions, expert reviews, or registry references. The extractors flag production claims for verification since sellers sometimes cite incorrect or misleading numbers to inflate perceived rarity.

Explain the difference between matching numbers and non-matching numbers vehicles and why it matters for valuation.

Matching numbers refers to a vehicle where the major drivetrain components — primarily the engine and transmission — still carry the original serial numbers or date codes that correspond to the vehicle's VIN and factory build records. A non-matching numbers car has had one or more of these components replaced at some point in its life, even if the replacement is the correct type.

This distinction is critically important in collector car valuation because it speaks to authenticity and provenance. A 1969 Chevrolet Camaro Z/28 with its original DZ 302 engine (verifiable by the engine stamp matching the VIN derivative and the correct date code for the assembly sequence) might be worth 40-60% more than an identical-looking Z/28 with a replacement 302. For some ultra-high-end cars, the premium is even larger — a matching-numbers Ferrari 250 GTO versus one with a replacement engine could represent millions of dollars in value difference.

The verification process involves checking stamped numbers on the engine block, transmission case, and sometimes the rear axle housing against factory records. For GM products, the Protect-O-Plate or cowl tag provides the reference. For Chrysler products, the fender tag and broadcast sheet serve this purpose. Ford products use door data plates and sometimes Marti Reports for build verification.

In Nuke, matching numbers status is tracked as a boolean field with supporting observation data. The extraction pipeline looks for explicit mentions in listings ('matching numbers,' 'numbers matching,' 'correct engine,' 'born-with drivetrain'). It also flags potential misrepresentation — when a listing claims matching numbers but the described engine specifications don't align with what was available for that year/model/option package.

There are important nuances. Some collectors care about matching numbers for the engine and transmission but are less concerned about the alternator or starter. Others want everything date-code correct, down to the hose clamps. The market also distinguishes between 'numbers matching' and 'date code correct' — the latter being a higher standard that requires components to have casting dates and assembly dates consistent with the vehicle's production timeline. Nuke captures the specific claims made and lets valuation models weight them appropriately based on the marque and era.

How does Nuke's import queue work and what are the different states a record can be in?

The import queue is the entry point for all new vehicle data entering the Nuke platform. Whether a vehicle is discovered through auction site scraping, Facebook Marketplace extraction, manual entry, or API submission, it first lands in the import_queue table before being processed into the vehicles table.

The import queue lifecycle follows a state machine pattern with these primary states: 'pending' means the record has been inserted but not yet picked up for processing. 'processing' means an edge function has claimed the record and is actively working on it — extracting data, deduplicating, validating. 'completed' means the record was successfully processed and a vehicle record was created or updated. 'failed' means processing encountered an error that prevented completion. 'duplicate' means the record was identified as matching an existing vehicle and was merged rather than creating a new entry. 'rejected' means the record was intentionally skipped, usually because it doesn't meet quality thresholds or isn't actually a vehicle listing.

The processing pipeline works as follows. When a new record enters as 'pending,' the import coordinator edge function picks it up within its polling cycle. It first performs deduplication by checking VIN (if available), URL, and fuzzy matching on year/make/model/location. If a match is found, the record is routed to the merge pathway rather than creating a new vehicle. If no match is found, the data is validated, normalized (make/model spelling, price formatting, date parsing), and inserted into the vehicles table. The import_queue record is then marked 'completed' with a reference to the created vehicle_id.

Locking is critical because multiple edge function instances may be running concurrently. The pipeline uses SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED to claim records, preventing two workers from processing the same import. A lock timeout of 30 minutes is enforced by the stale lock infrastructure — if a worker crashes mid-processing, the hourly stale lock release job will free the record for retry.

The pipeline_registry table documents which edge functions own which import_queue columns, preventing accidental writes from other parts of the system. Direct writes to import_queue outside the registered functions are discouraged and monitored. Common failure modes include malformed URLs, unparseable prices, and source sites returning anti-bot responses instead of actual listing data.

What are the key differences between American muscle cars and European sports cars from a data classification perspective?

From a data classification perspective, American muscle cars and European sports cars present distinct challenges that affect how Nuke categorizes, values, and presents vehicle data.

American muscle cars (roughly 1964-1974, with modern revivals) are characterized by large displacement V8 engines in mid-size bodies. Classification challenges include: the option code system is paramount — a base Chevelle and an SS 454 LS6 Chevelle share the same body but differ in value by 10-20x. The VIN and trim codes are essential for proper classification. Muscle cars were heavily optioned, and the specific combination of engine, transmission (4-speed manual vs automatic is a major value factor), rear axle ratio, and appearance packages creates thousands of permutations. Nuke must extract and store these option details because a 1970 Chevelle SS 396 with the L78 375hp engine and M21 close-ratio 4-speed is a fundamentally different vehicle from an SS 396 with the base L34 engine and automatic.

European sports cars follow different classification patterns. Italian exotics (Ferrari, Lamborghini, Maserati) are typically identified by model number and series rather than option codes — a Ferrari 308 GTB is a 308 GTB regardless of options, though QV (Quattrovalvole) versus non-QV is a significant variant. German cars (Porsche, BMW, Mercedes) use a more systematic model hierarchy where the model number encodes displacement and variant (a 911 Carrera RS 2.7 vs a 911 T are clearly distinguished). British cars (Jaguar, Aston Martin, Lotus) often use series designations (Series 1, Series 2, Series 3 E-Type) that carry huge value implications.

For pricing, muscle cars are more sensitive to documentation and numbers-matching status than many European cars. A Porsche 911 with a replacement engine is less penalized than a Hemi 'Cuda with a replacement block, because the Porsche community has historically been more accepting of mechanical rebuilds while the muscle car community prizes originality above all.

From an extraction standpoint, muscle car listings tend to be more detailed about drivetrain specifications and option codes, while European car listings emphasize service history, paintwork condition, and ownership provenance. Nuke's extractors are tuned to recognize these different patterns and weight their confidence accordingly based on the vehicle's classification category.

How does the YONO model handle hierarchical classification of vehicles?

YONO uses a hierarchical classification approach that mirrors how humans naturally identify vehicles — broad category first, then narrowing to specific identification. This is implemented as a multi-stage classification pipeline rather than a single flat classifier.

The first level classifies the image into a broad vehicle category: passenger car, truck/SUV, motorcycle, commercial vehicle, or non-vehicle. This initial filter is important because the training data includes many non-vehicle images from listing pages (interior shots of garages, paperwork photos, lifestyle images). Filtering these early prevents wasted computation in downstream classifiers.

The second level identifies the make (manufacturer). This is the highest-impact classification because make is the most reliable identifier and drives all subsequent narrowing. The model was trained on Nuke's own dataset of 33+ million images spanning hundreds of makes, giving it broad coverage including obscure manufacturers (Iso, Bizzarrini, Jensen, Monteverdi) that general-purpose vision models struggle with.

The third level predicts model within the identified make. This is where the hierarchy pays off — a Toyota classifier only needs to distinguish between ~50 model families rather than thousands across all makes. The model name prediction feeds into a lookup against Nuke's normalized model taxonomy to handle variants and regional naming differences.

The fourth level handles generation/year range estimation. This is the hardest visual task because year-to-year changes within a generation can be subtle (different grille texture, taillight shape, bumper style). YONO groups predictions into generation ranges rather than exact years for most vehicles, with exact year prediction reserved for cases where there are clear visual distinctions.

The Phase 5 architecture uses EfficientNet as the backbone, exported to ONNX for deployment flexibility. Inference runs at approximately 4ms per image on modern hardware, making it feasible to process the entire image backlog locally rather than relying on cloud vision APIs at $0.001-0.004 per image. The model is designed to be retrained incrementally as new labeled data becomes available, using the corrections and confirmations from human review as additional training signal.

The hierarchical approach also enables graceful degradation — if the model is confident about make but uncertain about model, it can return a partial classification rather than guessing, letting the observation system record what is known with appropriate confidence levels.

What is a barn find and how does it affect vehicle valuation in the collector market?

A barn find refers to a vehicle that has been stored away — often forgotten — for an extended period, typically decades, and is then rediscovered. The term originates from the literal discovery of cars in barns, but it extends to any long-term storage situation: garages, warehouses, shipping containers, or even open fields. The barn find phenomenon is one of the most emotionally compelling narratives in the collector car world.

From a valuation perspective, barn finds occupy a unique position. An untouched barn find with original paint, drivetrain, and interior — even if deteriorated — can sometimes command prices comparable to or exceeding fully restored examples. This is because the collector market has increasingly valued originality and patina over restoration quality. A 1967 Shelby GT350 pulled from a barn with original paint, numbers-matching drivetrain, and 12,000 original miles might sell for more than a concours-quality restoration of the same model, because the original car tells an authentic story that no restoration can replicate.

However, not all barn finds are valuable. The term is frequently misused in listings to romanticize what is actually a neglected, deteriorated vehicle with no special significance. A barn-found 1978 Chevrolet Malibu base model with a six-cylinder engine isn't a treasure — it's a parts car. The barn find premium only applies when the underlying vehicle is desirable and the unrestored condition preserves something meaningful (original paint, low mileage, rare options, documentation).

In Nuke's data model, barn find status is captured through multiple signals: condition descriptions mentioning long-term storage, low mileage relative to age, original paint claims, and explicit 'barn find' designations in listings. The extraction pipeline flags barn find claims for additional scrutiny because the term is so frequently misapplied for marketing purposes. A genuine barn find typically comes with supporting evidence — registration records showing when the car was last driven, photos showing the storage conditions, or provenance documentation explaining why the car was stored.

The valuation models treat barn find status as a modifier that interacts with the vehicle's base desirability, condition specifics, and the completeness of its originality. A barn find that's complete and numbers-matching gets a significant premium. A barn find that's missing its engine and has rust-through gets penalized despite the romantic narrative.

How does Nuke handle multi-source data reconciliation when different sources report conflicting information about the same vehicle?

Multi-source data reconciliation is one of Nuke's core architectural challenges. A single vehicle might appear on Bring a Trailer, Cars & Bids, Facebook Marketplace, and Craigslist with different descriptions, prices, photos, and even different claimed specifications. The observation system is designed specifically to handle this gracefully.

The fundamental principle is that Nuke never overwrites data — it accumulates observations. Each data point about a vehicle is stored as a separate observation record with its source, timestamp, confidence level, and the raw extracted value. When a BaT listing says a car has 45,000 miles and a Facebook listing of what appears to be the same car says 43,500 miles, both observations are preserved. The system doesn't pick a winner; it presents the evidence.

Reconciliation works through several mechanisms. First, source trust ranking: auction houses like RM Sotheby's and Bonhams get higher default confidence than marketplace listings because they perform verification. BaT benefits from community scrutiny — commenters frequently catch inaccuracies. Facebook Marketplace gets lower confidence because listings are unvetted. Second, recency: a 2026 observation supersedes a 2024 observation for fields that change over time (mileage, price, condition). Third, VIN-anchored data is treated as authoritative — if a VIN decode says the car is a 6-cylinder and a listing says V8, the VIN data wins for the factory specification (though the listing might be correct if an engine swap occurred).

The deduplication system uses a multi-factor matching algorithm: exact VIN match is definitive, but many vehicles lack VINs in their listings. Fuzzy matching considers year/make/model, geographic proximity, price similarity, photo fingerprinting, and seller information. When a potential duplicate is detected but confidence is below the auto-merge threshold, it's flagged for human review rather than automatically merged.

Conflict resolution surfaces in the API and UI as a confidence-weighted best estimate with the ability to drill into individual observations. For the SDK consumer, the top-level vehicle fields represent the highest-confidence values, while the full observation history is available for anyone who needs to understand the data lineage. This approach prevents the 'last write wins' problem that plagues simpler data architectures and ensures that even if a low-quality source temporarily provides incorrect data, the vehicle record retains the more reliable observations.

What is the significance of the Porsche 911 air-cooled versus water-cooled divide in the collector market?

The air-cooled versus water-cooled divide in the Porsche 911 lineup represents one of the most significant value inflection points in the entire collector car market. When Porsche transitioned from the air-cooled 993 generation (1994-1998) to the water-cooled 996 generation (1999-2004), it created a demarcation line that has driven enormous price divergence.

Air-cooled 911s (1964-1998) are powered by horizontally-opposed six-cylinder engines cooled by air rather than liquid coolant. This design was a defining characteristic of the 911 for 34 years and carries enormous emotional and mechanical significance for enthusiasts. The signature sound, the mechanical feel, and the engineering philosophy of the air-cooled flat-six are considered irreplaceable by purists.

The market reflects this sentiment dramatically. A 1998 Porsche 993 Carrera S (the last air-cooled 911) in good condition might sell for $120,000-180,000. A 1999 Porsche 996 Carrera of similar condition and mileage might sell for $25,000-40,000. That's a 4-5x price difference across a single model year transition. Earlier air-cooled cars command even larger premiums — long-hood 911s (1964-1973) have seen values increase 500-1000% over the past 15 years.

For Nuke's classification system, properly identifying air-cooled versus water-cooled status is critical for accurate valuation. The generation codes map clearly: 901/911 (1964-1989), 964 (1989-1994), and 993 (1994-1998) are air-cooled. The 996 (1999-2004), 997 (2005-2012), 991 (2012-2019), and 992 (2019-present) are water-cooled. But the extraction pipeline needs to handle common listing patterns where sellers emphasize 'air-cooled' as a selling point, sometimes incorrectly applying it to early water-cooled cars.

The market dynamics are also interesting from a data perspective. Air-cooled 911 prices appear to have plateaued for lower-specification models while continuing to climb for rare variants (RS, RSR, Turbo). Meanwhile, the 996 generation has begun appreciating as a new generation of buyers recognizes it as an undervalued entry point to 911 ownership. Tracking these trend divergences within a single model family is exactly the kind of analysis Nuke's historical pricing data enables.

How does Nuke's design system handle data-dense vehicle detail pages?

Nuke's design system was purpose-built for data-dense interfaces, drawing inspiration from motorsport telemetry displays and Bloomberg terminal aesthetics rather than consumer web design trends. Vehicle detail pages are the most complex views in the platform, combining dozens of data points across specifications, pricing history, images, provenance, and market comparisons.

The foundational rules are strict: Arial only (Courier New for data values), zero border-radius on all elements, zero shadows, zero gradients, 2px solid borders. This creates a deliberately utilitarian, high-information-density aesthetic that prioritizes readability over visual flourish. Labels are ALL CAPS at 8-9px, which allows dense labeling without visual noise.

For vehicle detail pages specifically, the layout uses a grid system that adapts to content volume. The primary identification block (year, make, model, VIN) sits at the top with the highest visual weight. Below that, the page divides into functional zones: specifications (engine, transmission, drivetrain, dimensions), condition and provenance (mileage, ownership history, documentation), pricing (current estimate, comparable sales, price history chart), and media (photos, documents).

Data values use Courier New to create visual alignment in specification tables — when engine displacement, horsepower, and torque values are displayed vertically, the monospaced font ensures the numbers align cleanly without needing padding tricks. This matters because vehicle specs often have varying digit counts (350 hp vs 1,001 hp) and the alignment aids rapid scanning.

The racing accent colors (Gulf blue/orange, Martini stripes, JPS black/gold, BRG, Papaya) are available as easter eggs but never used as primary UI colors. They might appear as subtle accent lines on vehicle cards for certain marques or as category indicators in specialized views, but the default palette is neutral grays and blacks with strategic use of color only for status indicators and data visualization.

Transitions are 180ms with a cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1) easing curve, giving interactions a snappy, mechanical feel rather than the soft, bouncy animations common in consumer apps. This timing feels intentional and precise, matching the platform's data-focused personality. The canonical CSS lives in src/styles/unified-design-system.css, with the older design-system.css frozen and no longer accepting changes.

What are the most common data quality issues with VIN decoding and how does Nuke handle them?

VIN decoding is fundamental to vehicle identification but comes with numerous data quality challenges that Nuke must handle systematically.

The most common issues include: First, transcription errors. VINs are 17 characters long and frequently mistyped in listings. The letters I, O, and Q are excluded from VINs by specification (to avoid confusion with 1, 0, and 9), but they regularly appear in manually entered data. Nuke's validation layer catches these immediately — any VIN containing I, O, or Q is flagged as invalid. The check digit (position 9) also provides error detection; Nuke validates this against the calculated value to catch transposition errors.

Second, pre-1981 VINs are non-standardized. Before the 17-character VIN was mandated in 1981, manufacturers used their own formats ranging from 5 to 13 characters. A 1970 Chevrolet Camaro VIN like '124870L512345' follows GM's format but cannot be decoded by standard VIN decoders. Nuke maintains marque-specific decoders for popular pre-1981 vehicles, but coverage is incomplete for obscure manufacturers.

Third, VIN cloning and fraud. Stolen vehicles sometimes carry VINs from legitimate vehicles, creating data conflicts when two seemingly identical vehicles appear in different markets. Nuke flags cases where the same VIN appears in geographically disparate locations simultaneously or where the VIN-decoded specifications don't match the visual evidence from photos.

Fourth, world manufacturer identifier (WMI) changes. When manufacturers change ownership or reorganize, their WMI codes can change. Chrysler vehicles made under Fiat Chrysler use different WMI codes than those made under DaimlerChrysler. This affects year-range filtering and make classification.

Fifth, incomplete VINs in listings. Many sellers, especially on marketplace platforms, redact portions of the VIN for security reasons, showing only the first few characters or the last six. Nuke stores partial VINs as-is and uses them for fuzzy matching during deduplication, but they cannot be validated or fully decoded.

Nuke's approach stores the raw VIN exactly as extracted, the decoded data from NHTSA and supplementary databases, validation status (valid, invalid, partial, pre-standard), and any discrepancies between decoded specifications and observed vehicle characteristics. The pipeline_registry ensures that only the designated VIN processing function writes to VIN-related fields, preventing other extractors from inadvertently corrupting validated VIN data.

How do seasonal patterns affect the collector car market and how does Nuke track these trends?

Seasonal patterns significantly influence the collector car market in predictable ways that Nuke's data reveals clearly when analyzing historical pricing trends.

The primary seasonal cycle follows the auction calendar. January kicks off with Scottsdale/Arizona auction week (Barrett-Jackson, Bonhams, RM Sotheby's, Gooding), which sets the tone for the year. Prices at Arizona often benchmark the market for the coming months. Spring sees Amelia Island auctions in March and various European sales. Summer brings Monterey/Pebble Beach week in August — the most prestigious auction event globally, where headline-making prices are achieved. Fall has Hershey (the massive AACA swap meet) and various smaller sales. This cycle means that the highest prices are typically achieved at Scottsdale and Monterey, with softer results at other times.

Beyond auctions, private sale and marketplace patterns follow different seasonality. Convertibles and roadsters see peak demand in spring and early summer as buyers anticipate driving season. Prices for open cars can be 10-15% higher in April-June compared to November-January. Conversely, closed cars (coupes, sedans) show less seasonal variation. Four-wheel-drive vehicles and trucks see demand spikes heading into winter in northern markets.

Geographic patterns overlay on seasonal ones. Florida and Arizona are year-round markets for collector cars. The Northeast and Midwest see compressed buying seasons (April-October) with winter slowdowns. California operates nearly year-round but sees peak activity around Monterey week.

Nuke tracks these patterns through its historical pricing data, storing sale dates alongside prices. The platform calculates rolling averages and seasonal adjustment factors for different vehicle categories. When presenting current valuations, the system can account for seasonal effects — showing both raw comparable sales and seasonally-adjusted estimates.

For the extraction pipeline, seasonality matters because auction frequency varies through the year, which affects data volume. The January and August auction weeks can produce thousands of new records in a few days, requiring the import queue and processing pipeline to handle burst capacity. Facebook Marketplace volume also fluctuates seasonally, with more private-party listings appearing in spring as people clean out garages and prepare cars for sale after winter storage.

What is a resto-mod and how does Nuke classify vehicles that blend original and modern components?

A resto-mod (restoration-modification) is a vehicle that has been restored cosmetically to period-correct or better-than-new appearance while being modified mechanically with modern components for improved performance, reliability, and drivability. Common resto-mod upgrades include fuel injection replacing carburetors, modern disc brakes replacing drums, updated suspension geometry, five or six-speed transmissions replacing three or four-speeds, and modern air conditioning systems.

Resto-mods present a classification challenge for Nuke because they don't fit neatly into traditional categories. A 1969 Camaro with an LS3 engine, Tremec 6-speed, Wilwood brakes, and Corvette C5 suspension is visually a 1969 Camaro but mechanically a modern vehicle. The factory specifications no longer apply, the matching-numbers concept is irrelevant, and the valuation model needs different inputs than a stock or restored example.

Nuke handles this through a modification taxonomy that tracks both the base vehicle identity and the specific modifications. The year/make/model reflect the body and chassis (1969 Chevrolet Camaro), while a modification_level field indicates the degree of departure from stock: stock, mildly modified, heavily modified, resto-mod, or pro-touring (a related but distinct category emphasizing high-performance handling). Specific modifications are stored as structured observations — engine swap details, brake upgrades, suspension changes — with sources and dates when available.

Valuation for resto-mods operates differently than for stock vehicles. Rather than comparing to other 1969 Camaros, the model considers the quality and reputation of the build (a Roadster Shop or Ring Brothers resto-mod commands premium over a home-garage build), the specific components used, the total investment documented, and comparable resto-mod sales regardless of the specific base vehicle. Build quality documentation — receipts, build photos, dyno sheets — significantly impacts resto-mod values.

The extraction pipeline identifies resto-mod characteristics through keyword detection (LS swap, Tremec, Wilwood, vintage air, RetroSound, Dakota Digital) and through specification inconsistencies (a 1965 Mustang listed with 500 horsepower clearly isn't stock). When resto-mod indicators are detected, the extractor adjusts its validation rules to avoid flagging legitimate modern specifications as errors. This prevents false positives where the system might otherwise reject a listing claiming a 1967 Chevelle has 650 horsepower and a 6-speed transmission.

Explain how Nuke's edge function architecture works with Supabase and why functions are being consolidated.

Nuke's edge functions run on Supabase's Deno-based edge runtime, which provides serverless execution close to the database. Each function is deployed as an independent endpoint that can be invoked via HTTP, triggered by database webhooks, or called on a cron schedule. The functions handle everything from data extraction and image processing to API endpoints and background pipeline tasks.

The architecture was originally designed with a microservice-inspired approach where each discrete task got its own function: one for BaT extraction, another for image optimization, another for VIN decoding, another for price normalization, and so on. This made sense initially for isolation and independent deployment, but it scaled to over 460 functions, creating serious operational problems.

The consolidation effort aims to reduce from 440 live functions to approximately 50. The problems with 440+ functions include: deployment takes too long because each function is individually built and deployed. Monitoring is overwhelming — checking logs across hundreds of functions makes debugging difficult. Many functions share nearly identical code (multiple extraction variants doing the same work with slight differences). Cold start times multiply across the fleet. And the cognitive overhead of understanding which function does what became untenable.

The consolidation strategy groups functions by domain. Instead of 15 separate extraction functions for different auction sites, a single extraction coordinator function handles routing based on the source URL. Instead of separate image processing functions for optimization, AI analysis, and organization, a unified image pipeline function handles all image operations. This reduces the function count while preserving the logical separation through internal routing.

Each consolidated function follows a coordinator pattern: it receives a request with an action parameter that routes to the appropriate internal handler. For example, the extraction coordinator might receive action='extract_bat', action='extract_pch', or action='extract_marketplace', each routing to specialized logic within the same function. This preserves the ability to deploy and test specific pathways while reducing the operational surface area.

The pipeline_registry table tracks which functions own which database fields, ensuring that even as functions are consolidated, the ownership model remains clear. When function A is absorbed into function B, the registry entries are updated to point to the new consolidated function. This prevents the consolidation from creating ambiguity about who writes what.

What makes the Toyota Land Cruiser FJ40 such a significant vehicle in the collector market?

The Toyota Land Cruiser FJ40 (1960-1984) has become one of the most sought-after collector vehicles globally, with prices rising dramatically over the past decade from the $20,000-40,000 range to $50,000-200,000+ for well-preserved or properly restored examples.

Several factors drive the FJ40's collectibility. First, genuine capability — the FJ40 was Toyota's answer to the Land Rover and Jeep, designed for military and commercial off-road use in the harshest environments. The inline-six engine (F and 2F variants) is legendarily reliable, and the vehicle's mechanical simplicity makes it maintainable indefinitely. This utility-first engineering gives it authenticity that purpose-built collector cars lack.

Second, global production and survival rates create interesting market dynamics. FJ40s were built in Japan and assembled in numerous countries including Brazil, Venezuela, and Costa Rica. Brazilian-assembled FJ40s are generally less valued than Japanese-built examples. Many FJ40s lived hard lives as work vehicles and were scrapped, making clean survivors increasingly rare. The attrition rate for these trucks was enormous — most were simply used up and discarded before the collector market recognized their value.

Third, the FJ40 appeals across collector demographics. Vintage car enthusiasts appreciate the mechanical character. Off-road enthusiasts value the capability. Design enthusiasts admire the proportions. And the lifestyle/outdoor culture has embraced the FJ40 as an icon alongside the Defender and early Bronco.

For Nuke's data model, FJ40s present specific classification challenges. The model designations encode engine and body information: FJ40 has the F-series engine, BJ40 has the B-series diesel, HJ40 has the H-series diesel. Wheelbase variants (FJ43 for medium, FJ45 for long) are distinct models with different values. Production records from Toyota are incomplete for many markets, making exact production numbers uncertain. Additionally, the FJ40 aftermarket is enormous — companies like Toyota Land Cruiser restoration specialists can build a complete vehicle from reproduction parts, raising authenticity questions.

The market has segmented into tiers: original, unrestored survivors command the highest prices when well-preserved. Frame-off restorations by reputable shops (like TLC, Red Line Land Cruisers) occupy the middle-upper tier. Modified/resto-modded examples with modern drivetrains form another segment. And project vehicles still trade actively for buyers willing to invest in restoration.

How does Nuke handle auction results where a car fails to sell, and why is that data valuable?

Auction no-sale results (also called 'passed,' 'not sold,' or 'bid to' results) are critical data points that Nuke captures and uses extensively, even though many data providers ignore them.

When a car fails to sell at auction, it means the bidding did not reach the seller's reserve price (or in a no-reserve auction, extremely rarely, received zero bids). The highest bid — the 'bid to' amount — reveals important market information: it establishes a ceiling that the market was unwilling to exceed for that specific vehicle at that specific time. This is arguably more informative than a sale result for understanding true market sentiment.

Nuke captures no-sale data with the same fidelity as completed sales. The vehicle record stores the auction_status as 'ended' (not 'sold'), the reserve_status as 'reserve_not_met,' and the highest bid amount in the bid_to or high_bid field. The extraction pipeline specifically looks for no-sale indicators: on BaT, the red 'Bid to $XX,XXX' banner; on RM Sotheby's, the 'Not Sold' designation with the high bid listed; on Mecum, the 'Bid To' annotation.

This data is valuable for several reasons. First, it prevents valuation overestimation. If you only look at completed sales, you get survivorship bias — you see what sold but not what failed. A model that shows three 1973 Porsche 911 Carrera RS sales at $500K-600K but ignores five that failed to sell at $450K-550K will overestimate market value. Second, no-sale data reveals market softness before price corrections become apparent in completed sales. When the no-sale rate for a particular model increases, it often precedes a downward price adjustment. Third, specific no-sale cases provide negotiation data — if a buyer sees that the same car failed to sell at a major auction, they have leverage in private negotiations.

Nuke tracks the no-sale rate as a market health metric: the percentage of vehicles offered at auction that fail to sell. Historically, healthy collector car markets see no-sale rates of 25-35%. When rates exceed 40%, it signals buyer caution. Rates below 20% suggest a hot market where demand exceeds supply. These macro metrics are calculated from Nuke's aggregated auction data and can be segmented by marque, era, price range, or auction house.

The challenge for the extraction pipeline is that no-sale results are less consistently formatted across platforms than completed sales. Some auction houses remove no-sale listings quickly, creating a time pressure on scraping. Nuke's scheduled extractors run shortly after auction events end to capture results before they potentially disappear.

What are the key considerations when extracting vehicle data from Facebook Marketplace listings?

Facebook Marketplace vehicle extraction presents unique challenges compared to auction sites, requiring specialized approaches at every stage of the pipeline.

Access is the first hurdle. Facebook's EDM (Enforcement and Detection Mechanisms) team has over 100 people dedicated to anti-scraping measures. Datacenter IP addresses are permanently blocked (error code 1675004). Only residential IPs work, and even those face rate limiting and fingerprinting. Nuke's breakthrough was discovering that the logged-out GraphQL API works without any authentication tokens — no LSD token, no dtsg token, no cookies needed. A POST to /api/graphql/ with doc_id=33269364996041474 returns marketplace listings as clean JSON.

Geographic fragmentation is both a challenge and an opportunity. Facebook Marketplace is organized by metro area, with roughly 500-1000 metros covering the US vehicle market. This means comprehensive coverage requires querying each metro individually, but it also means the data is naturally organized geographically — useful for understanding regional pricing patterns. The v2 scraper covers 55 major US metros.

Data quality from Facebook Marketplace is significantly lower than auction platforms. Sellers are casual — make and model might be misspelled, year might be approximate, prices might be placeholder values (like $1 or $123,456), and descriptions range from detailed to nonexistent. The extraction pipeline applies aggressive normalization: make names are matched against a canonical list (fixing 'Chevy' to 'Chevrolet,' 'Benz' to 'Mercedes-Benz'), prices below $100 or above $10M are flagged as likely invalid, and year values are validated against known production ranges for the identified make/model.

A critical technical detail: the GraphQL API's built-in year and make filters are broken — they're accepted in the query but silently ignored in results. All filtering must happen post-retrieval by parsing the listing title and attributes. This increases data transfer volume but ensures accurate filtering.

Pagination works correctly with the API, producing zero overlap across pages with a hard cap of 24 results per page. The scraper supports up to 50 pages per metro, yielding a maximum of 1,200 listings per city. In production testing across 5 cities, approximately 12% of all vehicle listings qualified as vintage/collector vehicles — a useful baseline for estimating national inventory.

Deduplication is particularly important for Facebook data because sellers frequently relist vehicles, and the same vehicle may appear in multiple nearby metro searches. Nuke's dedup system uses VIN matching when available, URL-based matching for relists, and fuzzy matching on year/make/model/price/location for cross-metro duplicates.

How does the collector car insurance market work and why is agreed value important?

Collector car insurance operates fundamentally differently from standard auto insurance, and understanding this distinction is important for Nuke's valuation services because insurance value is one of the primary use cases for vehicle appraisals.

Standard auto insurance uses actual cash value (ACV) — the insurer pays what the car is worth at the time of loss, accounting for depreciation. This works for daily drivers that depreciate predictably. But collector cars appreciate, and their value depends on subjective factors (originality, provenance, rarity) that standard depreciation models can't capture. If a standard insurer totals a 1967 Corvette, they might offer book value based on generic condition guides — potentially far below the car's actual market value.

Agreed value insurance solves this by establishing a specific insured value upfront. The owner and insurer agree that the vehicle is worth $150,000 (for example), backed by an appraisal or comparable sales data. If the car is totaled, the insurer pays $150,000 — no depreciation, no negotiation. This is why accurate, well-documented valuations matter so much.

Specialist collector car insurers (Hagerty, Grundy, American Collectors, Heacock Classic) offer these policies with additional benefits: spare parts coverage, flatbed-only towing, restoration coverage for cars mid-project, and automatic show/event coverage. Premiums are surprisingly low compared to standard insurance because collector cars are driven less, stored carefully, and owned by enthusiasts who maintain them meticulously — the loss ratio is excellent.

For Nuke, the insurance use case drives several product requirements. First, valuation documentation must be defensible — an insurer or claims adjuster needs to see comparable sales, condition assessments, and modification documentation to justify an agreed value. Nuke's observation system provides this by sourcing comparable transactions with dates, sources, and vehicle details. Second, regular revaluation is important because agreed values should be updated as the market moves — a car insured at $100,000 in 2020 might be worth $180,000 in 2026, leaving the owner significantly underinsured. Third, the platform needs to generate valuation reports that meet insurance industry standards, including comparable sales analysis, condition adjustment factors, and market trend context.

The connection between auction data and insurance valuation is direct: recent auction results for similar vehicles are the primary evidence used to establish agreed values. This makes Nuke's comprehensive auction data ingestion not just a market intelligence feature but a critical input to the insurance valuation workflow.

What is the observation system in Nuke and how does it differ from a traditional CRUD approach?

The observation system is Nuke's core data architecture pattern, and it represents a fundamental departure from traditional CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) approaches to vehicle data management.

In a traditional CRUD system, a vehicle record has fields like 'mileage,' 'price,' and 'color' that get overwritten when new information arrives. If a listing says the car has 45,000 miles today and a different source says 43,500 miles tomorrow, the second write replaces the first. The history is lost, the provenance is unknown, and there's no way to evaluate which value is more trustworthy.

Nuke's observation system treats each piece of information as an immutable observation — a fact claimed by a specific source at a specific time with a specific confidence level. The vehicle_observations table stores these observations with fields including: vehicle_id, field_name (what attribute is being observed), observed_value (the claimed value), source_id (which extraction source or human provided it), observed_at (when the observation was made), confidence (a 0-1 score reflecting source reliability), and raw_context (the surrounding text or data from which the observation was extracted).

This architecture provides several critical advantages. First, full data lineage — you can trace any vehicle attribute back to exactly where it came from, when, and with what certainty. This is essential for valuation defensibility and fraud detection. Second, conflict visibility — when two sources disagree about a vehicle's mileage, both observations exist and the conflict is visible rather than silently resolved by whichever source wrote last. Third, temporal accuracy — observations are timestamped, so you can see how a vehicle's data has evolved over time (price changes, mileage increases between listings, condition changes). Fourth, source quality measurement — by tracking observation accuracy over time, the system can adjust confidence weights for different sources based on their historical reliability.

The top-level vehicle fields (on the vehicles table) represent the current best estimate — the highest-confidence, most-recent observation for each attribute. These are materialized views of the observation data, updated by the pipeline when new observations arrive. But they're always backed by the full observation history.

The observation_sources table maintains a registry of known data sources with their default confidence levels. Auction houses get higher base confidence than marketplace listings. VIN decoders get near-perfect confidence for factory specifications. The YONO vision model gets confidence levels proportional to its classification certainty. This layered confidence system ensures that the materialized best-estimate values reflect the most trustworthy available information.

How does Nuke handle Japanese domestic market (JDM) vehicles and what special considerations apply?

Japanese domestic market (JDM) vehicles present unique data challenges for Nuke because they were manufactured for the Japanese market and often differ significantly from their USDM (US domestic market) counterparts, even when sharing the same model name.

The most fundamental difference is the VIN format. Japanese-market vehicles use a chassis number system rather than the standardized 17-character VIN used in North America and Europe since 1981. A JDM Nissan Skyline GT-R might have a chassis number like BNR32-123456, which encodes model (BNR32 = R32 GT-R), but doesn't follow the standard VIN decoding rules. Nuke's VIN validation must recognize these alternate formats and route them to JDM-specific decoders rather than rejecting them as invalid.

Model naming divergence is pervasive. The Toyota Supra is the JZA80 in Japan. The Nissan 240SX is the Silvia (S13/S14/S15). The Mazda Miata is the Eunos Roadster or MX-5. The Honda Civic Type R had JDM-exclusive variants (EK9, FD2) before the Type R came to the US. Nuke's model normalization system must handle these cross-market name mappings while preserving the original designation, since JDM collectors specifically want the Japanese-market version and use JDM model codes.

Specification differences between JDM and USDM versions of the same car can be substantial. JDM cars often had higher-output engines (due to different emissions regulations), right-hand drive, different suspension tuning, and unique trim levels. A JDM Mitsubishi Lancer Evolution might have features not available on the USDM version. The extraction pipeline needs to identify market origin and apply appropriate specification expectations.

The 25-year import rule is critical context. Under US federal law, foreign-market vehicles cannot be imported until they are 25 years old. This creates a rolling wave of newly-eligible JDM vehicles entering the US market each year. In 2026, 2001-model vehicles become eligible, which includes the R34 Skyline GT-R — one of the most anticipated eligibility dates in recent memory. Nuke tracks this eligibility timeline because it directly affects pricing: a JDM vehicle approaching its 25-year eligibility date typically sees accelerating price appreciation as US demand builds.

Valuation models for JDM vehicles must account for condition grading differences (Japan uses a detailed auction grade system: S, 6, 5, 4.5, 4, 3.5, 3, 2, 1, R, with sub-grades for interior and exterior), import and compliance costs, and the premium or discount associated with right-hand drive in left-hand-drive markets. Nuke ingests data from Japanese auction houses (USS, HAA, CAA) alongside US marketplace data to provide comprehensive JDM market coverage.

What is the role of provenance documentation in collector car valuation?

Provenance documentation is the paper trail that establishes a vehicle's history — who owned it, where it has been, what has been done to it, and how it has been cared for. In the collector car market, comprehensive provenance can add 20-50% to a vehicle's value, while absent provenance creates doubt and discounts.

The most valuable provenance documents include: the original window sticker (Monroney sticker), which confirms factory specifications and original options. The original title and registration history, showing an unbroken chain of ownership. Factory build sheets or broadcast sheets (for GM and Chrysler products), which detail every option ordered. Service records from authorized dealers, showing consistent maintenance. Receipts for restoration work, documenting the scope and quality of any rebuild. Period photographs showing the car in its original state. Competition history documentation for racing vehicles. Celebrity or historically significant ownership records.

In Nuke's data model, provenance information flows through the observation system like any other data point. Each document or claim is recorded as an observation with its source, and the cumulative provenance picture builds over time as more information is ingested from listings, auction descriptions, and registry data. The platform specifically tracks: number of documented owners, availability of original documentation, service history completeness, and any notable ownership or competition history.

The extraction pipeline identifies provenance indicators in listing text through pattern matching: phrases like 'original window sticker,' 'matching numbers verified by,' 'continuous service history from,' 'documented since new,' and 'accompanied by factory build sheet' all trigger provenance-related observation records. Photos of documents in listing galleries are flagged for OCR processing through the document_ocr_queue, which can extract specific data points from photographed paperwork.

Provenance is particularly important for pre-war vehicles, where the car's history may span 80-100 years. A 1930s Bugatti with documented racing history at Le Mans is worth multiples of one with unknown history, even if the cars are mechanically identical. For muscle cars, provenance helps verify authenticity — a claimed COPO Camaro needs documentation to prove it's genuine rather than a clone. For modern supercars, service history from authorized dealers confirms the car has been properly maintained, which is critical given the complexity and cost of servicing these vehicles.

How does Nuke's listing_page_snapshots table work and why does it consume so much storage?

The listing_page_snapshots table is one of Nuke's largest tables, consuming approximately 79 GB — roughly 46% of the total database size. It stores complete HTML snapshots of vehicle listing pages captured during extraction, serving as both an archive and a source-of-truth for data provenance.

When the extraction pipeline processes a listing URL, it first fetches the complete HTML content of the page and stores it in listing_page_snapshots before parsing. This snapshot serves multiple purposes: it provides a record of exactly what the listing page contained at extraction time (useful when sellers modify listings or when pages are taken down). It enables re-extraction — if the parsing logic improves, old snapshots can be reprocessed to extract additional data without re-fetching. And it provides evidence for data disputes — if a valuation is questioned, the original listing snapshot proves what was claimed.

The storage problem is straightforward: each listing page snapshot can be 100KB-2MB of HTML, and with hundreds of thousands of listings tracked, the table grows rapidly. Many snapshots are for listings that are no longer active — completed auctions, expired marketplace listings, sold vehicles. The HTML itself contains enormous amounts of boilerplate (navigation, footers, JavaScript, CSS) that isn't relevant to the extracted data.

The storage optimization plan involves several approaches. First, retention policy implementation — snapshots older than a configurable period (likely 6-12 months) for non-active listings can be archived to cold storage or deleted entirely, since the extracted observations are already preserved in the vehicle record. Second, content compression — storing gzipped HTML rather than raw text would reduce storage by roughly 70-80% for most pages. Third, selective storage — capturing only the relevant portion of the listing page (the listing content div) rather than the complete HTML with all site chrome.

This is identified as a Phase 4 optimization task in the platform triage plan. The tradeoff is between storage cost and re-extraction capability. Once a snapshot is deleted, re-extraction from that specific point in time becomes impossible. The recommended approach is to keep snapshots for active/recently-sold vehicles and implement an archival policy for older data, with the option to store compressed snapshots in Supabase Storage (object storage) rather than in the Postgres database where they consume expensive database storage.

What are the differences between concours, driver-quality, and project car condition levels?

Vehicle condition levels form a spectrum that dramatically affects valuation, and proper classification is one of the most impactful things Nuke's extraction pipeline does. The collector car market generally recognizes several condition tiers, with concours, driver-quality, and project representing the major categories.

Concours condition (also called 'show quality' or 'trailer queen') represents the highest possible standard. A concours-quality vehicle has been restored or preserved to factory-new or better-than-new condition. Every component is correct, properly finished, and period-appropriate. Paint is flawless, chrome is perfect, the engine bay is detailed, undercarriage is clean, and all trim, weatherstripping, and interior components are correct to the original specification. These vehicles are typically shown at competitive concours d'elegance events (Pebble Beach, Amelia Island, Villa d'Este) and may score 95-100 points on 100-point judging scales. Concours cars are rarely driven; they're transported on enclosed trailers and kept in climate-controlled environments. A concours-quality restoration of a significant vehicle might cost $200,000-500,000+ in labor and parts.

Driver-quality (also called 'tour quality,' 'driver,' or 'weekend driver') represents a vehicle in good to very good condition that is meant to be driven and enjoyed. It may have minor imperfections — a small paint chip, slightly worn driver's seat bolster, age-appropriate patina in hidden areas — but presents well overall and is mechanically sound. Driver-quality cars represent the sweet spot for many collectors: attractive enough to enjoy, not so precious that every stone chip causes anguish. These vehicles typically sell for 40-70% of concours-quality equivalents.

Project cars are vehicles requiring significant work — partial or complete restoration. Projects range from 'running and driving but needs cosmetics' to 'barn find requiring complete disassembly and rebuild.' Pricing depends heavily on completeness (is the correct engine present?), extent of rust or damage, rarity of the base vehicle, and parts availability. A rare project (like a genuine big-block Corvette or a Shelby Mustang) might still command substantial money because the base vehicle's value justifies the restoration investment. A common project (base-model sedan) might be nearly worthless.

Nuke's extraction pipeline classifies condition through NLP analysis of listing descriptions, photo assessment (via YONO for visual condition indicators), and explicit condition ratings when provided by auction houses. The confidence level of condition assessment varies significantly by source — a Bonhams catalogue description by a professional cataloguer is far more reliable than a Facebook Marketplace seller's self-assessment.

How does Nuke track and analyze the emerging electric vehicle (EV) collector market?

The emerging EV collector market presents a fascinating challenge for Nuke's data models because the traditional value drivers for collector vehicles (engine character, mechanical engagement, sound) don't apply, while new value drivers are emerging.

Early EVs are beginning to enter collector territory. The Tesla Roadster (2008-2012), based on the Lotus Elise platform, was the first highway-capable lithium-ion EV and is gaining collector interest as a historically significant vehicle. Early Nissan Leafs (2011-2012) have curiosity value as mass-market EV pioneers. The GM EV1 is among the most collectible EVs due to GM's infamous decision to crush nearly all of them — the few surviving examples are museum pieces. The original BMW i3 and i8 are architecturally innovative and starting to attract design-oriented collectors.

Nuke tracks EVs with the same observation system used for combustion vehicles, but with different specification fields gaining importance. Instead of engine displacement and horsepower, battery capacity (kWh), range (miles/km), motor output (kW), charging capability, and battery health/degradation percentage become the key specifications. Battery condition is the EV equivalent of engine condition — a degraded battery can cost $10,000-30,000+ to replace and dramatically affects both usability and value.

The classification system handles EVs through the drivetrain_type field (electric, hybrid, plug-in hybrid, fuel cell) and specific EV attributes. Battery technology type matters for longevity assessment — lithium iron phosphate (LFP) versus nickel manganese cobalt (NMC) versus older chemistries have different degradation profiles.

Market analysis for EV collectors requires different comparable sets. A 2012 Tesla Model S Signature edition isn't comparable to a 2024 Model S in the way a 1967 Corvette is comparable to a 2024 Corvette — the technology gap is too large. Instead, early EVs are compared within their technological cohort and valued based on historical significance, rarity, condition, and battery health.

Nuke's valuation models for EVs weight battery replacement status heavily — a vehicle with a recently replaced battery pack may be worth more than one with original but degraded cells, which is the inverse of the 'matching numbers' premium in the combustion collector market. This difference in value logic is built into the category-specific valuation pathways rather than applied universally.

What is Nuke's approach to handling vehicle image processing at scale?

Vehicle image processing is one of Nuke's most resource-intensive operations, with over 33 million images in the database and 32 million still pending AI analysis. The image pipeline handles optimization, classification, and organization through a multi-stage process.

The pipeline starts when images are ingested, either from listing scrapes (where images are downloaded from source URLs) or from direct uploads (like the iPhoto intake system for personal vehicle photos). Each image gets a record in the vehicle_images table with initial status fields: ai_processing_status='pending', optimization_status='pending', organization_status='unorganized'.

Optimization is the first processing stage. Source images vary wildly in quality and size — auction house photos might be 4000x3000 JPEG at 5MB, while Facebook Marketplace photos might be 800x600 at 50KB. The optimization pipeline creates standardized versions: a high-resolution version for detail viewing, a medium version for gallery display, and a thumbnail for list views. Images are stored in the vehicle-photos Supabase Storage bucket with a structured path: vehicle_id/image_id/size.webp. WebP format is used for better compression at equivalent quality.

AI analysis is the second stage, which is where YONO comes in. The vision model classifies each image along several dimensions: image type (exterior front, exterior rear, exterior side, interior, engine bay, detail shot, undercarriage, documentation, lifestyle/context), vehicle identification (make, model, year range), and condition indicators (paint quality, rust presence, modification detection). This classification data feeds back into the vehicle record as observations with the YONO source attribution.

The scale challenge is significant. At cloud API rates ($0.001-0.004 per image), processing 32 million pending images would cost $32,000-128,000. YONO's local inference at approximately 4ms per image and $0 marginal cost makes processing the full backlog feasible. The current deployment uses an ONNX-exported EfficientNet model that can run on CPU, though GPU acceleration is available for batch processing.

Organization is the third stage, where classified images are sequenced within the vehicle record to present the most useful viewing order: hero exterior shot first, then exterior angles, interior, engine bay, details, and documentation. The AI analysis results drive this ordering, and the organization_status tracks whether this sequencing has been applied.

The pipeline uses the same lock-based processing pattern as other Nuke queues — SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED with 30-minute stale lock timeout — to enable concurrent processing without conflicts. The ai_processing_status field moves through pending → processing → completed/failed/skipped, with failed records eligible for retry and skipped records (non-vehicle images, duplicates) excluded from further processing.

Explain the significance of the Ford Bronco in the collector market and its recent market trajectory.

The first-generation Ford Bronco (1966-1977) has experienced one of the most dramatic price appreciations in the collector car market, transforming from an affordable vintage truck to a six-figure collectible in roughly a decade.

In the early 2010s, clean first-gen Broncos could be purchased for $15,000-30,000. By 2020, the same trucks were selling for $80,000-150,000, and exceptional examples (particularly the rare half-cab models, early roadsters, and well-documented Bronco Sport packages) exceeded $200,000. This represents a roughly 5-8x appreciation — far outpacing most other collector vehicles in the same period.

Several factors drove this appreciation. The Bronco's simple, honest design resonates with contemporary aesthetic preferences — boxy, purposeful, unadorned. The vehicle's genuine off-road capability (short wheelbase, Dana 44 front axle, compact dimensions) gives it authentic credibility. The cultural association with outdoor lifestyle, surfing, and adventure brands aligned the Bronco with a demographic shift toward experiential vehicles. And Ford's announcement and subsequent release of the new Bronco (2021-present) renewed broad public awareness of the nameplate.

For Nuke's data classification, Broncos present interesting challenges. The model evolved significantly across its 11-year run: early Broncos (1966-1968) have a unique front end design and are more valued. The V8 engine option (302 or 289) commands significant premiums over the standard inline-six. Sport packages, which added cosmetic and comfort features, are worth identifying. And the half-cab body style (a shortened cargo area with a fold-down rear window) is rare and highly prized.

The Bronco market has also spawned a massive restoration and customization industry. Companies like Gateway Broncos, Velocity Restorations, Classic Ford Broncos, and ICON 4x4 produce fully rebuilt Broncos ranging from $150,000 to over $300,000. These restomod builds typically feature modern Ford crate engines (5.0 Coyote), automatic transmissions, upgraded suspension, air conditioning, and premium interiors while maintaining the classic exterior appearance.

Nuke tracks both original Broncos and these restomod builds as distinct market segments, since they serve different buyer motivations and follow different value trajectories. The original market is driven by authenticity and rarity. The restomod market is driven by build quality, builder reputation, and component specification. Understanding this distinction is critical for accurate valuation — comparing an original, unrestored Bronco to a $250,000 Velocity build would produce meaningless results.

How does Nuke's iPhoto intake system work for importing personal vehicle photos?

The iPhoto intake system allows Nuke users to import vehicle photos directly from Apple's Photos library, bridging personal photo collections with the platform's vehicle data system. The script at /Users/skylar/nuke/scripts/iphoto-intake.mjs provides the interface.

The system uses the osxphotos CLI tool (v0.75.1) to access the Photos library at the standard macOS location. Important limitation: only the CLI interface works — the Python API is not available, so all interactions go through shell commands. The script supports several modes: --list shows all vehicle albums, --album processes a specific album, --vehicle-id links photos to an existing vehicle record, and --all processes every vehicle album.

The workflow proceeds as follows. First, osxphotos enumerates photos in the specified album, handling the common issue of trailing spaces in album names (e.g., '1984 Chevrolet K20 LWB ' with a trailing space). Many photos may be stored in iCloud rather than locally, so the --download-missing flag triggers download of cloud-only originals before processing.

Each photo is uploaded to the vehicle-photos Supabase Storage bucket with metadata-rich path naming. The script creates vehicle_images records with source='iphoto' to distinguish these from web-scraped images. The observation_sources table includes an iphoto entry (id: 09cc8ecb-d268-4979-9e8d-ab1b680d54a2) for proper source attribution.

Once imported, the photos enter the standard image processing pipeline — they'll be optimized into multiple sizes, queued for YONO AI classification, and organized within the vehicle's image gallery. The AI pipeline processes these photos the same way it handles any other vehicle images, generating observations about vehicle condition, angle classifications, and feature identification.

A practical example: the 1984 Chevrolet K10 truck (vehicle_id: 6442df03-9cac-43a8-b89e-e4fb4c08ee99) had 419 photos imported from the '1984 Chevrolet K20 LWB ' album. These were uploaded and queued for AI analysis, though processing was paused globally via the NUKE_ANALYSIS_PAUSED flag at the time of import.

The system is designed for batch processing of personal vehicle documentation — walk-around photos, detail shots, documentation images, and progress photos for vehicles being restored. This personal photo data complements the web-scraped listing photos by providing comprehensive visual documentation that may not exist in any public listing.
